findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step (and corresponding AC) to inspect and, if needed, update the PyPI trusted publisher entry for this repo so it accepts OIDC tokens from the `workflow_dispatch`-on-`main` context (e.g., confirm the entry is environment-only-scoped or update it via pypi.org settings); document the before/after publisher configuration as part of the story's evidence.
evidence: 'The same workflow builds and then publishes using OIDC (`id-token: write`, the existing `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` trusted-publishing flow).'
'no intermediate tag push / no relay / no PAT'
ABSENCE: no step in the plan reads or updates the PyPI trusted publisher entry on pypi.org to reflect the trigger change from tag-push to workflow_dispatch

PyPA Trusted Publishing OIDC sub claim includes the triggering event context; changing from tag-push to dispatch changes the sub, which may cause PyPI to reject the token under the existing publisher entry
findings.finding: The plan switches the workflow trigger from `push: tags: v*` to `workflow_dispatch`, which changes the OIDC subject (`sub`) claim in the token PyPI receives. PyPA Trusted Publishing entries are tied to specific subject constraints (workflow filename + optional environment + optional ref/tag pattern). If the existing PyPI trusted publisher entry was configured against the tag-push context, the new dispatch-context OIDC token will be rejected by PyPI, silently breaking the only publish path. The plan never mentions checking or updating the PyPI trusted publisher configuration, so the union of described steps does not reach the stated end-state of a working OIDC publish.
findings.location: What section — 'Build, then publish DIRECTLY via PyPA Trusted Publishing' bullet; Scope — release.yml bullet; AC for OIDC publish
scenarios: The existing PyPI trusted publisher entry was registered against the tag-push workflow path; after the trigger change, the first real dispatch publish attempt fails with a 403 from PyPI even though all guards pass — no AC catches this before the story is closed.

Even if the current entry happens to be environment-scoped only (no trigger constraint), the plan provides no step to confirm this, so the executor has no way to verify the publish path is intact without a live test that the plan deliberately avoids.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope bullet: 'Verify (and update if necessary) the PyPI trusted publisher entry on pypi.org: confirm the entry's subject constraints are compatible with the `workflow_dispatch`-from-`main` OIDC token (e.g., environment-scoped without a tag-ref constraint). If it must be re-created, document the new entry parameters. Record the final entry configuration as evidence.' Add a corresponding AC: 'The PyPI trusted publisher entry for this repo is verified to accept `workflow_dispatch`-on-`main` tokens (environment-scoped, no conflicting ref constraint) — evidenced by the entry screenshot/API response recorded on the ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the required format for the `version` dispatch input (e.g., bare semver `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`, no `v` prefix) and specify that the workflow input definition should include a description or pattern constraint to reject malformed values at dispatch time.
evidence: 'A first job parses the intended version and every manifest and asserts exact equality before build: version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each server.json.packages[].version'
ABSENCE: no format specification or validation regex for the version input field in the workflow_dispatch inputs block
findings.finding: The `workflow_dispatch` version input has no stated format constraint or validation pattern (e.g., semver regex), so the lockstep step receives an arbitrarily-shaped string. The plan says the lockstep step 'parses the intended version and asserts exact equality' but never specifies what format the input must conform to before parsing. This is a minor defaultable gap: the executor will likely assume semver, but the plan should state it.
findings.location: What section — 'Version-lockstep, before build' bullet; AC 'release.yml is triggered by workflow_dispatch (version input)'
scenarios: The executor defines the input as a plain string with no pattern; a typo like '1.2' or 'v1.2.3' vs '1.2.3' passes the dispatch form but fails the lockstep check with a confusing error rather than an input-validation error.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the dispatch-trigger bullet: 'The `version` input is defined as a required string matching bare semver (`[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+`, no `v` prefix); the workflow input block includes a description to that effect.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the non-main-ref guard-firing test works given that `workflow_dispatch` uses the workflow file from the dispatched ref: specify either (a) creating a test branch containing the new workflow (with a clearly non-main name) to demonstrate the guard, or (b) replacing the non-main-ref test with a mismatched-version-only dispatch from main, noting that the ref guard is already proven by the workflow file's `if: github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'` check being codebase-verifiable.
evidence: 'after this story lands, the agent triggers `release.yml` via `gh workflow run` with (i) a **non-`main` ref**'
GitHub Actions: workflow_dispatch uses the workflow file from the dispatched ref, not from the default branch
The plan removes `push: tags: v*` and makes `workflow_dispatch` the only trigger, but does not specify creating a test branch containing the new workflow file
findings.finding: The guard-firing verification strategy (triggering `release.yml` with a non-main `--ref`) contradicts how GitHub Actions dispatches work: `gh workflow run --ref <non-main>` uses the workflow file from THAT ref, not from main. If non-main branches lack the new `workflow_dispatch` trigger (pre-existing branches), the dispatch fails at GitHub's level rather than at the guard step — the guard never fires, and the test proves nothing. The What and AC sections both describe this test approach without acknowledging this constraint.
findings.location: What section ('Guard-firing is verified AUTONOMOUSLY') and AC: 'the agent triggers release.yml via gh workflow run with (i) a non-main ref'
scenarios: Agent runs `gh workflow run release.yml --ref old-feature-branch` — GitHub returns 'workflow does not have workflow_dispatch trigger on that ref'; test fails to exercise the ref guard at all
Even with a specially-prepared test branch, the branch must have the guard logic, making the 'non-main' ref test a tautology unless carefully staged
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the non-main-ref dispatch test with: dispatch from main with only a mismatched version input to test the version-lockstep guard; note the ref guard is codebase-verifiable (the `if:` condition exists in the file) and is additionally covered by the environment's deployment-branch policy (which itself rejects non-main dispatch at the platform level). Remove the claim that the agent triggers with 'a non-main ref' as a guard-firing proof.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the near-duplicate 'retains F3/F4' ACs: keep one AC for pure structural retention (job names present via grep) and replace the second with a distinct AC specifically for the re-keying deliverable ('no `github.ref_name` or tag-`if:` version derivation remains; all version sources and job guards derive from `workflow_dispatch` input'), so the re-keying is not obscured as a repeat of the retention check.
evidence: AC bullet 2: 'The workflow **retains** F3's build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated `publish` job graph and F4's `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` split + evidence-consolidation job unchanged in structure (verified by grep…)'

findings.finding: Two separate ACs both require verifying that F3's and F4's job graphs are retained, with almost identical text. The second 'retains F3/F4' AC adds 're-keys the github_release/mcp_registry version sources + guards from tag context to workflow_dispatch input' — a real additional requirement — but the framing as a second 'retains' bullet obscures this, creating apparent near-duplication that could cause implementers to treat them as identical and miss the re-keying requirement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second and fourth bullet points
scenarios: Implementer reads AC 2, verifies job names present via grep, considers F3/F4 retention done, and skips the re-keying verification in AC 4 as 'already covered'
Reviewer counts two 'retains F3/F4' bullets and misses that the distinct deliverable is the re-keying step

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan to the plan: describe how to temporarily restore the ability to publish if the new dispatch workflow is broken (e.g., documented steps to re-add `push: tags: v*` via an emergency Gerrit CL, revert the environment re-key via the admin API, or designate a break-glass tag-based path with additional manual approval), so the removal of the tag-based path is reversible under incident conditions.
evidence: 'With the environment keyed to dispatch-from-`main`, a hand-pushed `v*` tag cannot reach the `pypi` environment and therefore cannot publish — this eliminates the… surface'
'Remove the `push: tags: v*` publish trigger (it can no longer reach the re-keyed environment)'
No rollback or back-out procedure described anywhere in the plan
The environment re-key is performed autonomously via admin API with no described revert step
findings.finding: The plan removes the `push: tags: v*` publication trigger and re-keys the `pypi` environment to dispatch-from-main — both destructive, irreversible changes to the release pipeline — but provides no rollback plan or back-out path. If the new dispatch-based workflow has a critical bug (e.g., misconfigured ref guard blocks all releases), there is no described procedure to temporarily revert to the tag-based path while a fix is prepared. The plan acknowledges the change is deliberate but T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism, not merely justification.
findings.location: What section ('No raw-tag publish path'), Scope ('.github/workflows/release.yml — Remove the push: tags: v* publish trigger')
scenarios: The new workflow has a bug in the ref guard (e.g., the condition is `!=` instead of `==`) that causes it to always abort from main, blocking all releases with no fallback
The pypi environment is re-keyed but the preflight step has a parsing bug, causing all releases to abort; the team needs to publish a security fix urgently
An upstream change to GitHub's deployment-branch-policy API silently alters the preflight response format, causing permanent release failure

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or a dedicated `docs/releasing.md` rationale section capturing *why* dispatch-from-main was chosen over tag-based triggering, why preflight-over-Terraform is the drift-detection approach, and the threat model it addresses — so the decision survives beyond this ticket.
evidence: AC says docs cover 'the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement' — rationale is absent
Plan's 'Why' section contains the decision rationale but the plan document itself will not live in the codebase
T5e(c): 'a novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update' — no such artifact is in scope
T7(a): 'A new pattern/contract/config/CLI gets a doc/ADR' — the new release-authorization pattern lacks a named ADR
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel release-authorization architecture (dispatch-from-main + env-preflight + guard helpers as a combined security control, replacing tag-based publishing) that constitutes a significant architectural decision, but the scoped documentation artifact is `docs/releasing.md` focused on process ('the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement'). No ADR or design-doc section capturing the *rationale* — why tag-based was abandoned, why preflight-over-Terraform was chosen, why OIDC/no-PAT — is required by any AC. Future maintainers will lack in-codebase context for undoing or evolving this decision safely.
findings.location: Scope / `docs/releasing.md` bullet; AC final bullet ('docs/releasing.md documents...')
scenarios: A future maintainer sees a re-keyed environment and dispatch-from-main trigger without understanding why tag-based was insecure, inadvertently re-introducing a tag trigger
Someone evaluating whether to add a TestPyPI path or a new tag trigger doesn't know the security rationale and makes an unsafe change
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section (e.g., 'Security rationale and decision record') to `docs/releasing.md` — or a new `docs/adr/release-authorization.md` — covering: (1) the threat (arbitrary-commit tag publish surface), (2) why dispatch-from-main eliminates it by construction, (3) why preflight > periodic TF drift for this use case, and (4) what would need to change to safely introduce a new publish path. Reference it from the process section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (and implement) how the preflight's compliance thresholds (minimum reviewer count, permitted branch name) are defined — either as explicit workflow-level env vars/inputs documented in `docs/releasing.md`, or as a clear statement that 'any non-zero reviewer count + main branch' is the permanent, intentionally minimal bar — so the rule is legible and intentionally evolvable rather than an implicit hardcode.
evidence: Plan says preflight 'fails the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not main-only' — the exact threshold (count, names) is unspecified and will land hardcoded in the step
T5e(b): 'rules/thresholds expected to evolve are configurable, not hardcoded'
Reviewer list and minimum count are governance parameters that change with team composition, not stable invariants
findings.finding: The preflight step's compliance thresholds — what constitutes 'required reviewers present' (minimum count, which reviewers) and 'main-only deployment-branch policy' — will be hardcoded in the workflow YAML step logic with no configurable representation. The plan specifies the check conceptually but does not address where the policy specification (e.g., 'at least 1 reviewer', 'branch = main') lives as a versioned, evolvable parameter. When reviewer policy evolves (e.g., requiring 2 reviewers, adding a new maintainer), the hardcoded check must be manually edited in `release.yml` and re-reviewed.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC — 'A release-time preflight step calls GET .../environments/pypi and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent or the deployment-branch policy is not main-only'
scenarios: Team adds a second required reviewer; preflight still passes with 1 reviewer because the count check is hardcoded to '>= 1'
A new maintainer is the only required reviewer; preflight passes even when that person is unavailable and can self-approve
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment block at the top of the preflight step (or a `env:` block in the job) that explicitly names the policy parameters (e.g., `REQUIRED_REVIEWER_MIN: 1`, `REQUIRED_BRANCH: main`) and reference the `docs/releasing.md` rationale section. This makes the threshold a named, grep-able, evolvable constant rather than an inline magic value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add retry-with-backoff (e.g. up to 3 attempts with exponential delay) to the preflight `GET /repos/.../environments/pypi` step and emit a structured log line distinguishing transient HTTP errors (5xx / rate-limit 429) from genuine policy-absent responses (200 with missing reviewers), so operators can tell a flaky API from a real misconfiguration.
evidence: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers' — no retry, backoff, or transient-vs-permanent error distinction mentioned
ABSENCE: no mention of HTTP status code inspection, retry loop, or timeout before treating the call as a genuine policy failure
The plan notes 'needs only the workflow's own read token' but does not address rate-limiting (the default GITHUB_TOKEN is subject to REST API rate limits during busy CI periods)
findings.finding: The preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` has no retry or backoff specified. A transient GitHub API error (5xx, rate-limit, network blip) is indistinguishable from a genuine misconfiguration, causing a spurious release abort with no operator-visible differentiation and no recovery path short of manually restarting the entire workflow dispatch.
findings.location: What section — preflight step / Acceptance Criteria: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed'
scenarios: GitHub API returns 503 during a busy release window → release aborts despite environment being correctly configured → team must re-trigger dispatch and wait for another reviewer approval
Rate-limit hit on GITHUB_TOKEN during a release run that already triggered many other API calls → spurious abort with no log distinction from real policy drift
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight shell step, wrap the `curl` call in a retry loop (e.g. `for i in 1 2 3; do response=$(curl -sf ...); status=$?; [ $status -eq 0 ] && break; sleep $((i*5)); done; [ $status -ne 0 ] && { echo 'ERROR: GitHub API unreachable after 3 attempts (transient?)'; exit 1; }`) and separately check the parsed JSON for missing reviewers (exit with a distinct message like 'ERROR: pypi environment policy absent — drift detected').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotency for the post-publish tag and GitHub Release creation steps (e.g. `git push --force-with-lease` with a check-then-create guard, or `gh release create --skip-if-exists`) and add a recovery note for the partial-failure scenario where PyPI already has the version but the tag/GH-Release is absent (e.g. the preflight checks for this condition and skips re-publish while still creating the missing tag/release).
evidence: 'once publish succeeds, the workflow creates the v* tag and the GitHub Release for the record/mirror' — no mention of what to do if this step fails
'the tag is an output, never a trigger' — correct, but no idempotency of tag creation on retry is addressed
ABSENCE: no mention of checking whether the tag already exists before creating it, or of a recovery runbook for 'PyPI has the version but the repo tag is missing'
PyPI's upload API returns HTTP 400 for duplicate filenames and HTTP 409 for duplicate versions — a retry after partial failure would fail at publish, not at the tag step
findings.finding: The plan creates a `v*` tag and GitHub Release only AFTER a successful publish, but does not specify idempotency or recovery for the partial-failure scenario where PyPI publish succeeds and tag/GH-Release creation then fails. A retry of the workflow_dispatch would attempt to re-publish to PyPI, which rejects duplicate versions (HTTP 400), leaving the release in an unrecoverable limbo: published to PyPI but with no corresponding tag or GitHub Release.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish' and 'Build, then publish DIRECTLY via PyPA Trusted Publishing — no tag relay'
scenarios: Publish to PyPI succeeds; `git push origin v1.2.3` fails (e.g. tag already exists from a stale run, or network error) → version is live on PyPI with no GitHub Release, no MCP registry entry, no evidence record

Operator retries the workflow_dispatch for the same version → publish step fails with PyPI 400/409 → workflow errors at publish rather than skipping to the post-publish steps → permanently stuck without manual intervention
findings.suggested_fix: Before the publish step, add a check: if `gh release view v$VERSION` succeeds, skip publish (already done) and proceed directly to post-publish steps. For tag creation, use `git tag -a v$VERSION ... || true` combined with `git push origin v$VERSION 2>&1 | grep -E 'already exists|Everything up-to-date' || exit 1` to make the step idempotent on retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the preflight step fails CLOSED on any API error (non-2xx status, timeout, unparseable response, 404) — not just on a 200-with-missing-reviewers — and add a corresponding unit-test fixture for an API-error response to the guard helper test suite.
evidence: Plan states: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not main-only'
No mention of what happens when the GET itself returns a non-2xx status, times out, or returns unparseable JSON — only the content-based failure case is specified.
The unit-test AC covers 'env-protection-absent JSON' but not a simulated API error response.
findings.finding: The preflight step's own API-call failure mode (network timeout, rate limit, 403/500 from GitHub) is unspecified — the plan states the step fails closed when the *response content* indicates missing reviewers, but does not state whether an API error (non-2xx, timeout, parse failure) also fails closed or silently passes, leaving the security gate in an indeterminate state.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent'
scenarios: GitHub API is temporarily unavailable (503) → preflight cannot read the environment config → undefined behavior; if the step exits 0 on curl/HTTP error, the release proceeds without the protection check, silently bypassing the gate.
Rate-limited (429) response during a high-activity period causes the preflight to fail transiently, blocking a legitimate release with no clear recovery path.
The environment was just created and the API returns 404 → step behavior unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight step implementation, treat any non-200 HTTP status or JSON parse failure as a hard failure (`exit 1`). In the unit-test AC, add a fourth fixture: 'API returns 500 → helper exits non-zero.' Document this fail-closed contract in the preflight step's inline comment in release.yml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring that the PyPA trusted publisher for `navapbc/rebar` on PyPI is verified (and updated if necessary) to accept OIDC tokens from the `workflow_dispatch` / `refs/heads/main` context (not just `refs/tags/v*`), and that this configuration is confirmed before the story is closed — either via a live TestPyPI publish dry-run or by recording the trusted-publisher subject-claim update as evidence.
evidence: "The positive clean-dispatch → real-PyPI publish is deliberately NOT triggered (publishing a real release is out of scope for verifying the story; the guard-PASS path is covered by the unit-tested helpers + the codebase-verifiable dispatch-from-main wiring)."

PyPA trusted publishers are configured per OIDC subject claim (e.g., `job_workflow_ref` + `ref`). Switching from a tag-triggered workflow (`refs/tags/v*`) to a dispatch-from-main workflow (`refs/heads/main`) requires updating the trusted publisher configuration on PyPI itself. No AC or 'What' step covers verifying or updating this configuration.
No named child ticket is cited to defer this live-boundary verification — the rubric's 'explicitly deferred to a NAMED child ticket' exemption does not apply.

OIDC subject claims differ between `push: tags` (ref = `refs/tags/vX.Y.Z`) and `workflow_dispatch` from main (ref = `refs/heads/main`); a trusted publisher accepting the former will reject the latter.
findings.finding: The newly-defaulted dispatch-from-main → OIDC → PyPI positive publish path is validated only through offline unit tests of guard helpers and codebase grep assertions, never against the live boundary. All ACs can be satisfied (unit tests pass, guard-firing confirmed on abort runs, environment configured) while the PyPA trusted publisher on PyPI is still configured for `refs/tags/v*` OIDC subject claims — meaning OIDC token issuance in the `refs/heads/main` dispatch context will be rejected by PyPI, silently breaking the actual release path the story is defaulting to.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — guard-firing AC and final AC block; 'What' section: 'Build, then publish DIRECTLY via PyPA Trusted Publishing'
scenarios: Story closes with all ACs green; first real dispatch-from-main release attempt fails with a PyPI 403 because the trusted publisher is configured for tag-ref OIDC claims.
A reviewer approves the publish environment step; OIDC token is issued for `refs/heads/main`; PyPI rejects it as no matching trusted publisher; the release fails after human approval is consumed.
The test suite and guard-firing runs all pass (they never reach the publish step), masking the broken live path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope item and AC: 'Update the PyPI (and optionally TestPyPI) trusted publisher configuration for navapbc/rebar to use the `workflow_dispatch`-compatible OIDC subject claim (`ref: refs/heads/main`, `job_workflow_ref` matching the new release.yml path). Verify by reading the trusted-publisher entry via the PyPI API or UI and recording it as evidence on the ticket.' This is a live configuration step the agent can perform autonomously (PyPI API or UI), analogous to the environment-config AC already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to scope: update the rationale comment in `test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` (lines 6-9 and 42) to reflect that tags stay open for record-keeping (post-publish tag output), not because release.yml depends on a pushed tag to trigger publishing.
evidence: Plan: 'the contract test `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` codifies "no tag lock". We do not restrict *who* can push a tag; publication safety now comes entirely from dispatch-from-`main`'

findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion asserts `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` 'codifies "no tag lock"' and stays unchanged. The test's PASS condition (no tag lock) is unchanged, but its stated *reason* is no longer correct once this plan lands, and the test contains a comment asserting 'reintroduce a tag lock' would be wrong because 'tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish to PyPI on a v* tag push' — which will be false after this story.

If the dispatched ref is a non-main branch that predates this story's changes (i.e., it does not yet contain the `workflow_dispatch` trigger in `release.yml`), GitHub will reject the API call at the dispatch layer ('workflow does not have workflow_dispatch trigger on this ref'), meaning the ref guard never fires — the test proves nothing about the guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `repo`-scoped PAT in the `pypi` environment with a fine-grained PAT (or GitHub App token) scoped to the minimum permission needed for environment administration (GitHub fine-grained PAT with `Administration: write` on `navapbc/rebar` only, or a GitHub App with the `environments: write` permission), and document the minimum-scope rationale in the AC.
evidence: Plan quote: 'the agent reads and, if drifted, configures the environment's required reviewers + `main`-only branch policy via the admin GitHub API (the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token)'

AC quote: 'autonomously by the agent using the environment's admin GitHub token: it reads GET /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi; ... configures the environment via PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi'
`repo` scope on a classic PAT grants full repository read/write; least-privilege would be a fine-grained PAT with only 'Administration: write' (or equivalent) permission scoped to navapbc/rebar
findings.finding: The plan states 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token' for the agent to configure the `pypi` GitHub environment via PUT API. A `repo`-scoped PAT is a long-lived credential that grants full read/write access to code, issues, pull requests, releases, secrets, and all repository content — far beyond the minimum needed for environment configuration (which only requires `repo` scope's `repo:admin` sub-scope, or in fine-grained PATs, just the 'Administration: write' permission on the repository). Storing a broad `repo`-scoped PAT in a GitHub environment means a compromised workflow job that can access environment secrets could exfiltrate a token capable of writing arbitrary code to the repo.
findings.location: What section / AC (live `pypi` environment config AC): 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token'
scenarios: A workflow run that accesses the `pypi` environment (e.g., a rogue dispatch or compromised reviewer account) could read the broad PAT and use it to write code to `main` via GitHub API, bypassing the Gerrit mirror lock.
If the PAT is accidentally logged or exposed in a workflow step, the blast radius is the entire repository rather than just environment management.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a GitHub fine-grained PAT with only `Administration: write` (repository scope, navapbc/rebar only) — this is sufficient for PUT /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{name}. Alternatively, use a GitHub App with the `Environments: read and write` permission, which is the narrowest machine identity for this task. Document the chosen identity and its scope in the AC and in docs/releasing.md.

findings.finding: The plan stores a `repo`-scoped admin token in the `pypi` GitHub environment for the agent to configure the environment via `PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi`, but a classic `repo`-scoped PAT grants full read/write access to code, secrets, issues, releases, and all repository content — vastly more than the 'Administration: write' permission (fine-grained PAT) that environment-management API calls actually require. This token lives in the `pypi` environment alongside `id-token: write` OIDC mint permissions; a compromised `publish` job (or a rogue required-reviewer approving a malicious dispatch run) could exfiltrate the PAT and use it to write arbitrary code to the repo via the GitHub API, bypassing the Gerrit mirror lock. The project's existing pattern (ADR 0011, `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md:516`) already uses fine-grained PATs scoped to `Administration: write` for repository administration — the plan is inconsistent with that established posture.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet: 'the environment holds a `repo`-scoped admin token'; AC (live `pypi` environment config): 'autonomously by the agent using the environment's admin GitHub token … configures the environment via PUT /repos/navapbc/rebar/environments/pypi'
scenarios: A malicious actor who gains `actions:write` on the repo and successfully dispatches a workflow run could, if a required reviewer approves (or if the reviewer gate is bypassed by the env-config drift this same plan tries to detect), access the broad `repo`-scoped PAT from within the `publish` job and write arbitrary code to the GitHub mirror — the Gerrit deploy-key lock protects `main` pushes from human actors but not API writes from a stolen token with full `repo` scope.
The broad PAT could be accidentally logged in a workflow step (e.g., the preflight curl call echoing response headers), giving any log viewer read access to a token with full repository write access.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and 'Human authorization' section, specify that the credential stored in the `pypi` environment for environment configuration is a **fine-grained PAT** with only `Administration: write` on `navapbc/rebar` (no `repo` scope, no `contents: write`, no `secrets: write`). This aligns with `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md:516` and ADR 0011, and limits the blast radius of a compromised `publish` job to environment-policy changes only — not arbitrary code writes.

PyPA Trusted Publishing OIDC sub claim format: if registered as environment-only (`repo:owner/repo:environment:pypi`), no update needed; if registered with a ref constraint (`repo:owner/repo:ref:refs/tags/v*`), dispatch tokens will be rejected — the plan never resolves this ambiguity
findings.finding: The plan switches the workflow trigger from `push: tags: v*` to `workflow_dispatch` but never declares or verifies the access posture of the existing PyPI Trusted Publisher entry. PyPA Trusted Publishing OIDC `sub` claims encode the triggering context (ref, environment, etc.); if the existing publisher entry carries a ref constraint tied to `refs/tags/v*`, PyPI will reject tokens issued from a `workflow_dispatch` run (which carries `ref: refs/heads/main` in the sub claim). The plan invokes 'the existing `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` trusted-publishing flow' without specifying whether it will accept dispatch-context tokens, and no AC requires checking or updating the PyPI publisher entry. This is an undeclared access posture on the pypi.org boundary-crossing path: the publish path may silently break at the first real dispatch publish attempt with no guard catching it before story closure.
findings.location: What section — 'Build, then publish DIRECTLY via PyPA Trusted Publishing' bullet; Scope — 'release.yml' bullet; AC: 'The workflow publishes directly via the OIDC trusted-publishing flow'
scenarios: The existing PyPI trusted publisher entry was registered against the tag-push context; after the trigger change, the first real dispatch publish attempt fails with a PyPI 403 even though all internal guards (ref guard, lockstep, ancestry, env preflight) pass — producing a failed release with no AC or guard catching it before the story is closed.

Even if the entry is environment-scoped only (which would work), the plan provides no step to confirm this, meaning the executor has no way to verify the positive publish path is intact without a live TestPyPI test that the plan explicitly excludes from scope.
